NCCN Guidelines Version 2.2026

Breast Cancer

คู่มือทบทวนสำหรับ General Surgery Board Exam — ครอบคลุมตั้งแต่ Diagnosis จนถึง Treatment

February 27, 2026 · Source: breast.pdf (NCCN Guidelines V2.2026)
Overview DCIS TNM Staging Workup Surgery Radiation Systemic Therapy Adjuvant IBC Special Situations Surveillance
1

Overview & Epidemiology

Key Facts (NCCN 2026)

Breast cancer is the most common malignancy in females in the United States and is second only to lung cancer as a cause of cancer death. The American Cancer Society estimated 313,510 Americans will be diagnosed and 42,780 will die of breast cancer in the US in 2024.

Source: Discussion Section MS-2, NCCN Guidelines V2.2026

Molecular Subtypes

HR+/HER2−

Most common subtype. Responds to endocrine therapy. Low likelihood of pCR with chemotherapy. Use gene expression assays (e.g., Oncotype DX) for risk stratification.

Molecular Subtypes

HR−/HER2+

HER2-amplified. Treat with HER2-targeted therapy (Trastuzumab ± Pertuzumab). High likelihood of pCR with neoadjuvant chemo + HER2 blockade.

Molecular Subtypes

Triple Negative (TNBC)

ER−, PR−, HER2−. Most aggressive. Highest likelihood of pCR with neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Consider Pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-522). Check germline BRCA mutation.

Biomarker Testing (BINV-A)

  • ER testing: All invasive breast cancers. ER+ = 1%–100% IHC staining. ER-low-positive (1–10%) — heterogeneous group, behavior similar to ER-negative; individualize decision.
  • PR testing: Required for all invasive cancers. PR ≥1% = positive.
  • HER2 testing: Required for all invasive cancers by ASCO/CAP guidelines.
  • Ki-67: Test if HR+/HER2− and considering adjuvant abemaciclib.
  • HER2 in DCIS: NOT recommended — does not alter management.
BINV-A, Discussion MS-10–11
2

Ductal Carcinoma In Situ (DCIS)

Definition

DCIS = Tis, N0, M0. Non-invasive cancer confined to ducts. Over 50,000 cases diagnosed in the US per year (2022 data). The goal of primary therapy is to prevent progression to invasive carcinoma. Choice of local treatment does NOT impact overall disease-related survival.

Discussion MS-3 to MS-9

Workup for DCIS

  • History & physical examination
  • Bilateral diagnostic mammography
  • Pathology review
  • ER status determination (for adjuvant endocrine therapy)
  • MRI — only in select circumstances (controversial)
  • Genetic counseling if high-risk hereditary
  • Assess for distress (NCCN Distress Thermometer)
DCIS-1, Discussion MS-3

Treatment Options for DCIS

  • BCS + WBRT ± boost Category 1 — reduces IBTR by 50–70%
  • Total mastectomy ± SLNB Category 2A ± reconstruction
  • BCS + APBI Category 2A — selected patients (ASTRO "suitable")
  • BCS alone Category 2B — only if very low risk, ER+, margin ≥2mm
Discussion MS-6 to MS-7

Margin Status for DCIS

  • For DCIS treated with BCS + WBRT: margin ≥2 mm associated with low IBTR
  • For DCIS treated with BCS alone (no WBRT): optimal margin width unknown; recommend ≥2 mm, some evidence for wider margins
  • DCIS + microinvasion (≤1 mm): use DCIS margin definition (≥2 mm)
Discussion MS-5, MS-6; BINV-F

SLNB Indications in DCIS

  • Mastectomy for DCIS → strongly consider SLNB
  • Excision in anatomic location compromising future SLNB performance
  • Oncoplastic breast-conserving surgery
  • NOT recommended for pure DCIS undergoing BCS (unless concern for invasion/axillary mets)
  • Omission of SLNB at mastectomy may be considered: patients >50 years, non-palpable low-grade ER+ DCIS, no abnormal axillary findings, mastectomy for patient preference
DCIS-1, footnotes h & i; Discussion MS-6

Adjuvant Endocrine Therapy for DCIS

  • ER+ DCIS after BCT: Tamoxifen (pre- and postmenopausal) OR AI (postmenopausal, especially <60 yr) Cat 1 with RT
  • Standard tamoxifen: 20 mg/day × 5 years
  • Low-dose tamoxifen (5 mg × 3 yr) — only if standard dose not tolerated
  • Anastrozole = non-inferior to tamoxifen in IBIS-II and NSABP B-35 (postmenopausal)
  • NSABP B-24: tamoxifen ↓ IBTR by 3.4% absolute; no OS difference
Discussion MS-8 to MS-9

Follow-up after DCIS

  • Interval H&P every 6–12 months × 5 years, then annually
  • Yearly diagnostic mammography
  • After BCT: first mammogram 6–12 months after completing RT Cat 2B
  • ~50% of local recurrences are invasive in nature
DCIS-2, Discussion MS-9
3

TNM Staging (AJCC 8th Edition)

Note: The Anatomic Stage Group table should only be used in global regions where biomarker tests are not routinely available. Cancer registries in the US must use Clinical and Pathological Prognostic Stage Group tables.

ST-3

T Classification

TDefinition
TisDCIS (or Paget disease without invasive cancer)
T1mi≤1 mm
T1a>1 mm but ≤5 mm
T1b>5 mm but ≤10 mm
T1c>10 mm but ≤20 mm
T2>20 mm but ≤50 mm
T3>50 mm
T4aExtension to chest wall
T4bUlceration / ipsilateral satellite nodules / peau d'orange
T4cT4a + T4b
T4dInflammatory carcinoma (IBC)

AJCC Anatomic Stage Groups

StageTNM
0TisN0M0
IAT1N0M0
IBT0–T1N1miM0
IIAT0–T1/T2N1/N0M0
IIBT2–T3N1/N0M0
IIIAT0–T3N2/N1M0
IIIBT4N0–N2M0
IIICAny TN3M0
IVAny TAny NM1
ST-1 to ST-3, AJCC 8th Edition

Key N Classification Points

  • cN1mi: Micrometastases — approximately 200 cells, >0.2 mm but ≤2.0 mm
  • cN1: Metastases to movable ipsilateral level I, II axillary nodes
  • cN2: Fixed/matted axillary nodes OR internal mammary nodes (without axillary mets)
  • cN3: Infraclavicular, internal mammary + axillary, OR supraclavicular nodes
  • pN1mi: >0.2 mm but ≤2.0 mm; pN1a: 1–3 nodes (≥1 >2.0 mm); pN2a: 4–9 nodes; pN3a: ≥10 nodes
  • LCIS — removed from TNM staging (8th edition)
ST-2 to ST-3
4

Workup for Invasive Breast Cancer

Initial Workup (BINV-1)

  • History & physical examination
  • Bilateral diagnostic mammography
  • Breast ultrasound (as necessary)
  • Axillary ultrasound (if considering omission of surgical nodal staging)
  • Pathology review (ER, PR, HER2, Ki-67 if applicable)
  • Distress assessment
  • Genetic counseling (if high-risk criteria met)
  • Fertility discussion (premenopausal patients)
BINV-1, Discussion MS-10

Additional Metastatic Workup

NOT routinely recommended for early-stage asymptomatic patients.

  • Bone scan / Na-F PET/CT: bone pain or elevated ALP
  • CT chest (diagnostic): pulmonary symptoms
  • CT abdomen/pelvis or MRI: elevated ALP, abnormal LFTs, abdominal symptoms
  • FDG-PET/CT: equivocal standard imaging; NOT routine for stage I–II or operable III
  • Metastases by bone scan in 5.1% (stage I), 5.6% (stage II), 14% (stage III)
Discussion MS-12

Role of MRI in Workup

  • MRI is optional — not universally recommended
  • Indications: staging extent in dense breasts, multifocal/multicentric disease, axillary mets with occult primary, Paget disease, lifetime risk >20%, prior mammographically undetected cancers
  • 2 prospective randomized trials showed no improvement in re-excision rates after BCS with preoperative MRI
  • MRI alone NOT sufficient to determine if BCT is optimal — tissue sampling still needed
  • Dedicated breast coil + experienced team required if performed
Discussion MS-10 to MS-11
5

Surgical Treatment

Key Principle

Multiple randomized trials document that mastectomy is equivalent to BCT (BCS + WBRT) with respect to OS as primary treatment for the majority of stage I and II breast cancers Category 1. Optimal choice = shared decision between patient and clinician.

Discussion MS-13

Breast-Conserving Surgery (BCS)

Absolute Contraindications

  • Pregnant requiring RT during pregnancy
  • Diffuse suspicious/malignant microcalcifications on mammography
  • Widespread disease — cannot be resected with satisfactory cosmetic result
  • Diffusely positive pathologic margins
  • Homozygous ATM mutation (biallelic inactivation) Cat 2B

Relative Contraindications

  • Prior RT to breast/chest wall
  • Active connective tissue disease (scleroderma, lupus)
  • Persistently positive pathologic margin
  • Known/suspected genetic predisposition (BRCA1/2)
Discussion MS-13 to MS-14, BINV-G

Margin Assessment

Invasive cancer: Positive margin = "ink on tumor" (any invasive cancer or DCIS on ink). Standard per SSO/ASTRO guidelines.

DCIS component: ≥2 mm margin associated with reduced IBTR.

Positive margin after BCS → re-excision to achieve "no ink on tumor" OR mastectomy.

Discussion MS-14
Figure: NCCN Algorithm — Locoregional Treatment: Mastectomy ± PMRT (BINV-3) | NCCN Guidelines V2.2026

Axillary Management

  • SLNB first-line for clinically node-negative patients (cN0)
  • ALND omission: Based on SOUND and INSEMA trials — patients >50 yr, postmenopausal, cT1N0 (node-negative by axillary ultrasound), HR+/HER2−, grade 1–2, agreeable to WBRT + endocrine therapy → may omit SLNB Cat 2A (caution in lobular histology)
  • ALND: Indicated if SLNB positive and not meeting low-risk criteria
  • Preoperative systemic therapy: If cN+ → may use SLNB alone if node-negative after therapy (NSABP B-51 trial criteria)
BINV-1 footnote b; BINV-D; Discussion MS-13

Contralateral Risk-Reducing Mastectomy

  • No absolute survival benefit for most patients with unilateral breast cancer
  • May reduce breast cancer-specific mortality only in young patients (18–49 yr) with stage I/II ER-negative disease (5-yr survival 88.5% vs 83.7%) — but selection bias likely
  • Discouraged by NCCN Panel except for patients with known/suspected genetic predisposition
  • Patients ≤35 yr or premenopausal carriers of BRCA1/2 mutation → consider additional risk-reduction strategies after counseling
Discussion MS-14 to MS-15
6

Radiation Therapy

WBRT after BCS

  • Reduces 10-yr recurrence risk (19% vs 35%; RR 0.52) and 15-yr breast cancer death (21% vs 25%; RR 0.82)
  • Preferred dose (hypofractionated): 40–42.5 Gy in 15–16 fractions Preferred
  • Conventional: 46–50 Gy in 23–25 fractions (select patients)
  • Ultra-hypofractionated: 28.5 Gy in 5 weekly fractions — pTis/T1/T2/N0, age >50 yr Cat 2A
  • RT boost: 10–16 Gy in 4–8 fractions; recommended for high-risk (age <50, high-grade, focally positive margins)
  • RT omission: >70 yr, ER+, T1, cN0 + 5 yr tamoxifen/AI — acceptable Cat 1
Discussion MS-18 to MS-22, BINV-I

PMRT (Post-Mastectomy RT)

  • ≥4 positive nodes: PMRT to chest wall + comprehensive RNI Category 1
  • 1–3 positive nodes: Strongly consider PMRT to chest wall + comprehensive RNI Category 1
  • pN0, pT3 (>5 cm): Consider chest wall RT ± RNI
  • pN0, ≤pT2, negative margins ≥1 mm: PMRT typically NOT recommended
  • High-risk features in node-negative: central/medial tumor, pT3, or tumor ≥2 cm + <10 nodes + grade 3/ER−/LVI → consider PMRT + RNI
  • Positive margins: If re-resection not possible → strongly consider PMRT ± RNI
Discussion MS-23 to MS-25, BINV-3

APBI (Accelerated Partial Breast Irradiation)

  • NCCN recommends APBI for BRCA-negative patients meeting ASTRO 2016 "suitable" criteria:
  • Age >50 yr + ER+ invasive ductal carcinoma + ≤2 cm (pT1) + margins ≥2 mm + no LVI
  • Also permits APBI: age >50 yr, screen-detected low/intermediate-grade DCIS ≤2.5 cm, margins ≥3 mm
  • Preferred regimen: 30 Gy in 5 fractions every other day (University of Florence, IMRT) Preferred
  • NSABP B-39: APBI IBTR 4.6% vs WBRT 3.9% (absolute difference 0.7%) — considered not clinically significant in selected patients
Discussion MS-20 to MS-21, BINV-I; BINV-2

RT Sequencing

  • If chemo + RT both indicated: adjuvant RT typically after completion of chemotherapy
  • CMF + RT — may be given concurrently
  • Endocrine therapy: sequential or concurrent with RT acceptable; initiation at completion of RT may be preferred
  • Capecitabine (radiosensitizer) — give after completion of adjuvant RT
  • Olaparib — give after completion of RT (OlympiA trial protocol)
  • HER2-targeted therapy — may be given concurrently with RT
Discussion MS-24 to MS-25
7

Preoperative Systemic Therapy

Key Principle

Randomized trials show no difference in long-term outcomes when systemic chemotherapy is given before or after surgery. Preoperative therapy improves breast conservation rates, provides prognostic pCR information, and allows testing novel therapies.

Discussion MS-30

Indications for Preoperative Therapy

Inoperable (always indicated):

  • Locally advanced / IBC
  • Bulky or matted cN2 axillary nodes
  • cN3 regional nodal disease
  • cT4 tumors

Operable (preferred approach):

  • TNBC: clinical stage T2N0 or higher, or node-positive
  • HER2+ disease: clinical stage T2N0 or higher, or node-positive
  • Patient desires BCS but tumor large relative to breast size
  • High likelihood of response based on subtype
Discussion MS-30 to MS-31

Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy Key Trials

  • KEYNOTE-522 (TNBC): Pembrolizumab + chemo → 5-yr EFS 81.3% vs 72.3% (placebo). ↓ risk recurrence 37% (HR 0.63). Cat 1, Preferred
  • TRYPHAENA (HER2+): Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab + chemo → pCR 57–66%
  • NeoSphere (HER2+): Adding pertuzumab to T+docetaxel ↑ pCR, improved outcomes in node-positive
  • pCR = strongest with TNBC, less so HER2+, least with HR+
  • Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) is prognostic within each subtype (HR 1.55–2.16 per RCB increase)
Discussion MS-31 to MS-32
8

Adjuvant Systemic Therapy

Endocrine Therapy for HR+ Disease

  • Tamoxifen (pre- and postmenopausal): ↓ annual odds recurrence 41%, ↓ annual odds death 31% (vs no ET, regardless of chemo/age/ALN)
  • 5 years > 1–2 years (prospective RCTs)
  • 10 yr tamoxifen (ATLAS/aTTom): further ↓ recurrence (risk 21.4% vs 25.1% at yr 5–14) — but ↑ risk endometrial ca + PE
  • AI (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane): Postmenopausal only. Initial AI > tamoxifen for DFS (ATAC: HR 0.85 for DFS). NO OS difference.
  • Sequential AI after tamoxifen: ITA, IES, ABCSG8/ARNO95 — all show improved EFS/OS
  • AI not active in patients with functioning ovaries
  • No meaningful differences in efficacy/toxicity between the 3 available AIs
Discussion MS-33 to MS-36

Ovarian Function Suppression (OFS)

  • Achieved by: GnRH agonist (goserelin/leuprolide), oophorectomy, or ovarian irradiation
  • Consider in premenopausal with high recurrence risk (young age, high grade, node+)
  • TEXT + SOFT trials: Exemestane + OFS vs Tamoxifen + OFS → DFS 92.8% vs 88.8% (HR 0.66; p<0.001). No OS difference.
  • 13-yr follow-up (TEXT-SOFT): OS benefit for OFS + exemestane in high-risk patients only
  • ASTRRA trial: Tamoxifen + OFS (2 yr) → 8-yr DFS 85.4% vs 80.2% (tamoxifen alone) in <45 yr + prior chemo
Discussion MS-36 to MS-37
Figure: NCCN Algorithm — Systemic Adjuvant Treatment: HR+/HER2− Disease, Premenopausal, pT1–3 pN0 (BINV-7) | NCCN Guidelines V2.2026
Figure: NCCN Algorithm — Systemic Adjuvant Treatment: HR−/HER2+ Disease (BINV-9) | NCCN Guidelines V2.2026

HER2+ Adjuvant Therapy

  • pT1c–T3, pN+: Chemo + Pertuzumab + Trastuzumab Cat 1, Preferred
  • pT1b–T1c, pN0: Chemo + Trastuzumab Cat 1
  • pT1a, pN0: Consider chemo + Trastuzumab Cat 2B (uncertain for very small tumors)
  • APHINITY trial (11.3-yr F/U): Adding Pertuzumab to T + chemo ↓ recurrences (especially in node+)
  • Residual disease after neoadjuvant HER2 therapy → T-DM1 (KATHERINE)
BINV-9, BINV-5; Discussion (adjuvant section)

Gene Expression Assays

  • Oncotype DX (21-gene RS): pT1–T2, HR+, HER2−, pN0 or pN1 (1–3 nodes)
  • RS 0–25 (premenopausal) or 0–25 (postmenopausal) → endocrine therapy alone (TAILORx data)
  • RS ≥26 → chemotherapy + endocrine therapy
  • RS 16–25 in premenopausal: adding chemo ↓ distant recurrence (especially young age)
  • BCI, EndoPredict, Prosigna (PAM50) also accepted for risk assessment
  • For pN1 (1–3 nodes): optimized BCI model may aid in extended ET decision
BINV-N, BINV-6 to BINV-8
9

Inflammatory Breast Cancer (IBC)

Definition (Clinical Diagnosis)

IBC is a rare, aggressive form accounting for 1–6% of breast cancers. Requires erythema AND dermal edema (peau d'orange) of ≥⅓ of the breast skin. Primary tumor classified as T4d by definition. Stage: IIIB, IIIC, or IV depending on nodal involvement and distant mets.

Dermal lymphatic involvement NOT required nor sufficient alone to diagnose IBC. Differential: cellulitis, mastitis.

Discussion MS-82; IBC-1

Features of IBC

  • Usually HR-negative
  • More frequently HER2+ than usual ductal cancers
  • All molecular subtypes exist, but basal and HER2-overexpressed are most frequent
  • More likely to present at younger age
  • Less favorable prognosis compared to non-inflammatory locally advanced BC
Discussion MS-82

Workup for IBC

  • CBC, platelet count
  • Pathology review + ER, PR, HER2 status
  • Bilateral diagnostic mammogram ± ultrasound
  • Breast MRI (optional)
  • LFTs, bone scan or Na-F PET/CT Cat 2B
  • CT chest/abdomen/pelvis Cat 2B
  • FDG-PET/CT Cat 2B — helpful for equivocal findings
Discussion MS-82 to MS-83

Treatment of IBC — Combined Modality Approach

  1. Preoperative systemic therapy first — anthracycline-based ± taxane. Always before surgery.
  2. HER2+ IBC: Add trastuzumab ± pertuzumab to chemo. Pertuzumab-containing regimen permitted preoperatively.
  3. Surgery: Modified radical mastectomy (MRM) with level I/II ALND if response. BCS NOT recommended for IBC. SLNB not reliable in IBC.
  4. Delayed breast reconstruction — standard after mastectomy for IBC. Immediate reconstruction not recommended (need for skin resection, RT outcomes).
  5. Radiation: After mastectomy + completion of planned chemo. RT to chest wall + supraclavicular region. ± internal mammary nodes if clinically/pathologically involved.
  6. HR+ IBC: Add endocrine therapy sequentially after preoperative systemic therapy.
Discussion MS-83 to MS-84
10

Special Situations

Paget Disease of the Breast

  • Rare manifestation — neoplastic cells in NAC epidermis
  • Presents with eczema, bleeding, ulceration, itching of nipple
  • Associated cancer elsewhere in breast in 80–90% of cases
  • Biopsy: full-thickness skin of NAC (including at least portion of clinically involved NAC)
  • When biopsy positive → breast MRI recommended
  • Treatment: Traditionally total mastectomy; BCS with NAC excision + WBRT acceptable
  • Adjuvant systemic therapy based on stage/HR status
  • Paget with ER+ DCIS + breast conservation → consider tamoxifen
Discussion MS-77 to MS-78

Phyllodes Tumor

  • Rare — stromal + epithelial elements. Benign, borderline, malignant subtypes
  • Mean age 40 yr (older than fibroadenoma, younger than invasive ca)
  • FNA/core needle biopsy inadequate to distinguish from fibroadenoma
  • Treatment: Local excision with tumor-free margins ≥1 cm. Prefer lumpectomy/partial mastectomy
  • Total mastectomy only if negative margins cannot be obtained
  • Rarely metastasize to ALNs → SLNB/ALND NOT necessary (unless pathologic LN clinically)
  • No proven role for endocrine therapy or adjuvant chemo
  • Distant recurrence → usually lung; treat per NCCN Soft Tissue Sarcoma guidelines
Discussion MS-79

Breast Cancer During Pregnancy

  • Rate: 1.3 per 10,000 live births (California registry)
  • Usually ALN-positive, larger primary, poorly differentiated, ER/PR−; ~30% HER2+
  • Mammogram with shielding: accuracy >80%; ultrasound: abnormal in up to 100%
  • Staging: minimize fetal radiation exposure
  • Chemo NOT in first trimester; RT NOT during any trimester
  • BCS possible if RT can be delayed to postpartum
  • SLNB: insufficient data — decisions individualized
  • When surgery at ≥25 wks: obstetrical team must be onsite
Discussion MS-80 to MS-81; PREG-1

Axillary Breast Cancer (Occult Primary)

  • Adenocarcinoma + positive axillary nodes ± mediastinal nodes → highly suggestive breast primary
  • Workup: mammogram + breast US + breast MRI (identifies primary in ~50–70%)
  • Test ER/PR, HER2 (elevated ER/PR = strong evidence for breast primary)
  • Treatment options: mastectomy + ALND OR ALND + whole-breast RT ± systemic therapy
  • MRI can help guide BCS vs mastectomy decision in occult primary
Discussion MS-85
11

Surveillance & Follow-up

Interval Recommended Notes
Years 1–5 H&P every 6–12 months Annual diagnostic mammography
Year 5+ H&P annually Annual diagnostic mammography
After BCT First mammogram 6–12 months post-RT Cat 2B
Endocrine therapy Monitor per NCCN Breast Cancer Risk Reduction guidelines Gynecologic symptoms if on tamoxifen
Tumor markers NOT recommended for routine surveillance No benefit shown for monitoring relapse
CBC / LFTs Not routinely recommended Only if candidate for systemic therapy
Bone density (DEXA) Monitor if on AI or OFS Consider bisphosphonate for high-risk node-negative or node-positive postmenopausal patients
BINV-17, DCIS-2; Discussion MS-52

Monitoring Metastatic Disease (BINV-R)

  • Baseline imaging before initiation of new therapy
  • Monitor effectiveness of cytotoxic chemo and endocrine therapy
  • Assessment when evidence of disease progression
  • Use same method of response assessment over time (e.g., same CT protocol)
  • CTC/ctDNA: NOT yet included in NCCN Guidelines for disease assessment/monitoring in metastatic breast cancer
  • Frequency can be reduced in patients with long-term stable disease (based on clinical judgment)
Discussion MS-75 to MS-76

ข้อมูลจาก: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology — Breast Cancer, Version 2.2026 (February 27, 2026)
